title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: Let y1:ny_{1:n} ordered sequence. segmentation mm changepoints ordered set τ0:m+1\tau_{0:m+1} 0=τ0<τ1<…<τm<τm+1=n0=\tau_0<\tau_1<\dots<\tau_m<\tau_{m+1}=n, partitioning data m+1m+1 contiguous segments. Within segment data governed parameter θj\theta_j (e.g. mean, variance, distribution) changes τj\tau_j(Truong et al. 2020). offline methods minimise penalised cost ∑j=1m+1𝒞(y(τj−1+1):τj)+βf(m), \sum_{j=1}^{m+1} \mathcal{C}(y_{(\tau_{j-1}+1):\tau_j}) + \beta f(m), 𝒞(⋅)\mathcal{C}(\cdot) segment cost (typically −2×-2 \times maximised log-likelihood) βf(m)\beta f(m) guards -segmentation (Yao 1988). choice penalty β\beta central challenge field.

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: methods available cpt_detect() release fall three broad families: ggcpt_compare() function runs multiple methods series arranges results. respects future::plan() optional parallel execution many methods compared.

what: Comparing methods on a simulated series
title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: Generate three-regime series changes mean variance: faceted layout shows method panel, x-axes aligned:  overlay layout draws changepoints single panel, colour-coded:  numeric summary, ggcpt_compare_table() returns tidy tibble:
code: set.seed(2022) x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 10, 1), rnorm(100, 5, 2)) ggcpt_compare(x, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "facet") ggcpt_compare(x, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "overlay") ggcpt_compare_table(x, methods = cmp_methods) #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>    method    cp cp_value #>    <chr>  <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 pelt     100    0.467 #>  2 pelt     200   10.3   #>  3 binseg   100    0.467 #>  4 binseg   200   10.3   #>  5 fpop     100    0.467 #>  6 fpop     200   10.3   #>  7 fpop     247    3.83  #>  8 fpop     251    8.22  #>  9 fpop     284    2.81  #> 10 fpop     287    8.76

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: true changepoint locations known, cpt_metrics() provides standard suite accuracy measures (Truong et al. 2020; van den Burg Williams 2020):
code: truth <- c(100, 200)  # PELT res_pelt <- cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "meanvar") generics::tidy(res_pelt)$cp #> [1] 100 200  # Metrics for BinSeg res_binseg <- cpt_detect(x, method = "binseg", change_in = "meanvar") cpt_metrics(generics::tidy(res_binseg)$cp, truth, n = length(x)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl>

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: ggcpt_eval() overlays predictions ground truth, colouring true positives, false positives, misses within tolerance margin:
code: ggcpt_eval(pred = c(100), truth = c(100, 200), data_vec = x)

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: now conduct systematic comparison across five built-test signals (Donoho Johnstone 1994), using tolerance margin 5 observations. table shows different methods perform across signal types. PELT FPOP, exact methods, generally strong performance, WBS can detect small short features exact methods miss default penalty setting—may also -segment.
code: set.seed(42) signals <- list(   blocks = signal_blocks(512),   fms    = signal_fms(512),   mix    = signal_mix(512),   teeth  = signal_teeth(512),   stairs = signal_stairs(512) ) methods <- c("pelt", "binseg", "fpop", "wbs", "not") margin <- 5  results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(signals), function(nm) {   sig <- signals[[nm]]   truth <- attr(sig, "true_changepoints")   do.call(rbind, lapply(methods, function(m) {     res <- tryCatch(       cpt_detect(sig$value, method = m, change_in = "mean"),       error = function(e) NULL     )     if (is.null(res)) return(NULL)     pred <- generics::tidy(res)$cp     met <- cpt_metrics(pred, truth, n = length(sig$value), margin = margin)     data.frame(signal = nm, method = m, met)   })) })) #> Warning in BINSEG(sumstat, pen = pen.value, title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: Precision / Recall / F1: standard classification measures margin tolerance. Covering: average Jaccard overlap true predicted segments (van den Burg Williams 2020). Hausdorff distance: worst-case localisation error predicted true changepoint sets.

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: complete reproducible workflow combining simulator, detection, evaluation:
code: set.seed(1) dat <- cpt_simulate(200, changepoints = c(60, 120),                     change_in = "mean", params = c(0, 8, 3), sd = 1) truth <- attr(dat, "true_changepoints")  res <- cpt_detect(dat$value, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean") pred <- generics::tidy(res)$cp  cpt_metrics(pred, truth, n = length(dat$value)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   200      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl>

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: multiple ground-truth annotations available—Turing Change Point Dataset (van den Burg Williams 2020)—use cpt_metrics_annotated() average metrics across annotators:
code: cpt_metrics_annotated(pred = c(100, 200),                       annotations = list(c(100, 200), c(105, 198)),                       n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_annotators n_pred precision recall    f1 covering #>   <dbl>        <int>  <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1   300            2      2         1      1     1    0.977

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: future future.apply packages installed, ggcpt_compare() evaluation loop can run methods parallel setting future::plan(): Parallel execution reproducible: seed supplied, detectors fanned parallel-safe L’Ecuyer-CMRG random streams, result identical regardless worker count backend.
code: future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 2) ggcpt_compare(x, methods = c("pelt", "binseg", "wbs", "fpop", "not"),               seed = 1)

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: vignette("introduction", package = "ggchangepoint") comprehensive walkthrough package API supported methods. R package references individual detection engines: changepoint (Killick Eckley 2014), wbs (Fryzlewicz 2014), (Baranowski et al. 2019), mosum (Eichinger Kirch 2018), fpop (Maidstone et al. 2017), IDetect (Anastasiou Fryzlewicz 2022), breakfast (Fryzlewicz 2020, 2022), changepoint.np (Haynes et al. 2017), ecp (James Matteson 2014).

title: ggchangepoint feature tour
text: vignette gives quick tour every major feature ggchangepoint. See vignette("introduction") detailed walkthrough vignette("comparison") method comparison.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggchangepoint R package provides unified, tidy interface changepoint detection across multiple algorithmic backends. introduces ggcpt S3 result class broom-style methods (tidy(), glance(), augment()), central cpt_detect() dispatcher supporting dozen detection algorithms, native ggplot2 visualization via autoplot() specialised geoms, method comparison accuracy evaluation modules, data simulation framework canonical test signals. harmonising disparate APIs existing R changepoint packages behind single convention, ggchangepoint lowers barrier exploratory changepoint analysis reproducible method comparison.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: Changepoint detection—problem identifying points sequence underlying statistical properties change—fundamental task time series analysis (Truong et al. 2020; Aminikhanghahi Cook 2017). applications across virtually every domain involves sequential data, including genomics (Picard et al. 2005), finance (Athey et al. 2022), climate science (Haslett Raftery 1989), signal processing (Lavielle 2005). R ecosystem offers rich set changepoint packages, implementing one detection algorithms conventions input, output, parameterisation. changepoint package (Killick Eckley 2014) provides PELT (Killick et al. 2012), Binary Segmentation (Scott Knott 1974; Vostrikova 1981), Segmented Neighbourhood, AMOC. wbs (Fryzlewicz 2014) breakfast packages implement Wild Binary Segmentation variants, (Baranowski et al. 2019), mosum (Eichinger Kirch 2018), fpop (Maidstone et al. 2017), IDetect (Anastasiou Fryzlewicz 2022), others offer specialised algorithms. nonparametric side, changepoint.np (Haynes et al. 2017) ecp (James Matteson 2014; Matteson James 2014) handle distributional changes. diversity strength R community, creates practical difficulties analyst. package uses different result class, different naming conventions parameters, different plot methods, different changepoint indexing conventions. Comparing output several detectors data—standard practice robust analysis—requires user write manual conversion code. Furthermore, none existing packages natively produce ggplot2 (Wickham 2016) graphics support broom (Robinson 2017) convention tidy data extraction. ggchangepoint addresses problems providing single, consistent interface wraps widely used detection packages. design goals : Uniformity: single ggcpt result class regardless underlying detection engine, broom-style methods tidy data access. Discoverability: central cpt_detect() dispatcher whose documentation lists supported methods capabilities. Visualisation: first-class ggplot2 integration autoplot() specialised geoms. Comparison evaluation: built-tools running multiple detectors, tabulating results, computing accuracy metrics, visualising discrepancies. Reproducibility: simulation framework generates data known changepoints, enabling rigorous benchmarking.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggcpt class S3 class stores complete output changepoint detection structured format. Every detection function ggchangepoint—whether called cpt_detect() directly—returns ggcpt object, ensuring uniform interface downstream processing. ggcpt object named list following components: changepoints: tibble detected changepoint locations (cp) corresponding data values (cp_value). segments: tibble describing fitted segments (segment ID, start, end, length, segment-level parameter estimate). data: original data series tidy tibble index value. metadata fields: method, change_in, penalty, fit (raw upstream object), call, cp_convention. cp_convention component records whether changepoint indices follow left-segment convention—last observation change, used changepoint package—right-segment convention. ggchangepoint methods report locations left-segment convention, results packages use alternative convention (e.g., ecp) normalised automatically methods can compared common footing.
code: library(ggchangepoint) library(ggplot2) library(generics) theme_set(theme_light())  set.seed(2022) x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 10, 1))  res <- cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean") class(res) #> [1] "ggcpt" print(res) #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          pelt  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         MBIC = NA  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467 res$cp_convention #> [1] "left"

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ground-truth changepoint locations known—either synthetic data labelled data set—ggchangepoint provides comprehensive suite accuracy metrics cpt_metrics(): Precision recall based margin tolerance (default 5). F1 score, harmonic mean precision recall. Covering metric, length-weighted average Jaccard overlap true predicted segmentations (van den Burg Williams 2020). Hausdorff distance sets predicted true changepoint locations. Adjusted Rand index, measuring agreement induced segment labellings (Hariz et al. 2007). Annotation error, absolute difference predicted true number changepoints (van den Burg Williams 2020). Mean absolute error (MAE) root mean squared error (RMSE) changepoint locations. tolerance margin, detections within margin considered correct: scenarios multiple ground-truth annotations, cpt_metrics_annotated() computes metrics annotator averages: ggcpt_eval() function provides visual evaluation plot, showing true predicted changepoints colour-coded match status:
code: cpt_metrics(pred = c(100, 200), truth = c(100, 200), n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl> cpt_metrics(pred = c(105, 205), truth = c(100, 200), n = 300, margin = 10) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1    0.936         5      0.903 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl> cpt_metrics_annotated(pred = c(100, 200),                       annotations = list(c(100, 200), c(105, 198)),                       n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_annotators n_pred precision recall    f1 covering #>   <dbl>        <int>  <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1   300            2      2         1      1     1    0.977 ggcpt_eval(pred = c(100, 200), truth = c(100, 200), data_vec = x)

what: Case Study: Comparative Evaluation on a Block Signal
title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: illustrate complete workflow—simulation, detection, comparison, evaluation—using Blocks test signal added Gaussian noise: Detect changepoints every method available build, score known truth tolerance margin 5, collect results single table: Visual evaluation PELT result, ±5\pm 5 tolerance windows shaded predictions coloured match status:
code: set.seed(1) sig <- signal_blocks(512) truth <- attr(sig, "true_changepoints")  x_noisy <- sig$value + rnorm(512, 0, 0.5) methods_cs <- c("pelt", "binseg", "amoc") if (has_fpop) methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "fpop") if (has_wbs)  methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "wbs") if (has_not)  methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "not")  metrics <- do.call(rbind, lapply(methods_cs, function(m) {   res  <- cpt_detect(x_noisy, method = m, change_in = "mean")   pred <- generics::tidy(res)$cp   data.frame(method = m, cpt_metrics(pred, truth, n = 512, margin = 5)) })) metrics[, c("method", "n_pred", "precision", "recall", "f1", "covering")] #>   method n_pred precision     recall        f1  covering #> 1   pelt     11         1 1.00000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 #> 2 binseg      5         1 0.45454545 0.6250000 0.6019535 #> 3   amoc      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.3128671 #> 4   fpop     11         1 1.00000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 #> 5    wbs     11         1 1.00000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 #> 6    not     11         1 1.00000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 pred_pelt <- generics::tidy(cpt_detect(x_noisy, method = "pelt"))$cp ggcpt_eval(pred = pred_pelt, truth = truth, data_vec = x_noisy)

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggchangepoint provides unified, tidy interface diverse changepoint detection ecosystem R. standardising single result class, adopting broom conventions, integrating natively ggplot2, package reduces friction exploratory changepoint analysis facilitates reproducible method comparison. Planned directions future development include: Additional wrapper coverage: integration detection packages strucchange, segmented, bcp. Online detection: support streaming sequential changepoint detection. Model selection helpers: visual tools penalty selection, including elbow plots cross-validated loss curves. Multivariate high-dimensional methods: improved handling multivariate changepoint detection, leveraging ecp’s existing multi-dimensional support. Interactive visualisation: integration interactive plotting frameworks exploratory data analysis. Contributions bug reports welcome package’s GitHub repository (https://github.com/PursuitOfDataScience/ggchangepoint).

title: Combines Changepoint Analysis with ggplot2
text: package provides composable ggplot2 layers changepoint visualisation:
code: library(ggplot2)  # Use geom_changepoint as a standalone layer cp_tbl <- tidy(cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean")) ggplot(data.frame(index = seq_along(x), value = x), aes(index, value)) +   geom_line() +   geom_changepoint(data = cp_tbl, aes(xintercept = cp), color = "red") +   theme_ggcpt()  # Use stat_changepoint to compute and draw changepoints in one step ggplot(data.frame(index = seq_along(x), value = x), aes(index, value)) +   geom_line() +   stat_changepoint(method = "pelt", color = "red")  # Shade alternating segments between changepoints ggplot(data.frame(index = seq_along(x), value = x), aes(index, value)) +   geom_line() +   annotate_segments(cp = cp_tbl$cp, n = length(x))  # Highlight segments with geom_cpt_segment ggplot(data.frame(index = seq_along(x), value = x), aes(index, value)) +   geom_line() +   geom_cpt_segment(data = cp_tbl, aes(xintercept = cp), color = "blue")  # Draw confidence intervals with geom_cpt_ci (when the engine provides them) ggplot(data.frame(index = seq_along(x), value = x), aes(index, value)) +   geom_line() +   geom_cpt_ci(data = cp_tbl, aes(xintercept = cp, ymin = lower, ymax = upper))

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: ggchangepoint 0.2.0 CRAN. turned three-engine 0.1.0 prototype unified, tidy, ggplot2-native framework: single S3 result class (ggcpt) broom-style tidy()/glance()/augment() methods, autoplot() method, family composable geoms (geom_changepoint(), geom_cpt_segment(), geom_cpt_ci(), stat_changepoint()), unified cpt_detect() dispatcher, first-wave wrappers WBS, WBS2, , MOSUM, FPOP, Isolate-Detect TGUH, method-comparison module optional future parallelism, accuracy-metrics module, data simulator canonical benchmark signals, thorough hardening original four functions. document plans next release. two things 0.2.0 cycle finish, come first matter users today: closes documentation coverage gap. package now exports ~37 user-facing functions, README, package-level help, even vignettes introduce subset; documentation actively mis-states surface (README advertises gfpop engine wrapped; package-level help still says package “include[s] three changepoint packages”; cpt_detect()’s signature advertises 13 methods error “yet implemented”). §2 makes first-class, blocking deliverable next release introduce every feature package actually provides — README, vignette, runnable example — reconcile every - -claim. package ships features front door never mentions package whose features exist users. makes dispatcher honest finishes foundation (§3): wire stop advertising stub methods, add cpt_methods() introspection helper, deliver genuine multivariate input instead silently flattening , extend opt-future parallelism beyond ggcpt_compare(). top plans next engine wave (§4: gfpop, SMUCE/HSMUCE, CROPS penalty paths, robust loss, DeCAFS, self-normalisation), surveys remaining method backlog mathematics citations (§5: Bayesian, high-dimensional/multivariate, regression breaks, online/sequential, kernel), — answering brief think harder build — proposes set genuinely new, non-detector features (§6: completed S3 surface, time/date ts/xts/tsibble input, panel/batch detection, interactive Shiny exploration, predict()/segment labelling, stability diagnostics, reproducible citations, decomposition--detect, interop/export). document closes visualization, evaluation, simulation extensions, architecture/dependency/testing plan, phased release schedule, record 0.2.0 fixed, verified bug audit reproducing twelve concrete defects found actually running 0.2.0 (§11.3 — including broken canonical test signal, accuracy metric returns recall > 1, stat misplaces changepoints dated axes), complete, verified reference list. two commitments define package unchanged: () every detector returns tidy tibble inside structured ggcpt object, (ii) every result directly renderable ggplot2.

what: 1.3 Design principles (carried forward, with one addition)
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: P1 — Wrap, don’t reinvent. Bind peer-reviewed, CRAN-published engines; new engines enter Suggests, guarded requireNamespace(). P2 — Tidy , tidy . Every detector returns ggcpt; column names stable across methods. P3 — ggplot2 way . Every result renders autoplot() extends +. P4 — One vocabulary. consistent argument set (x, method, penalty, min_size, n_cpts, …); method-specific arguments flow .... P5 — Progressive disclosure. Beginners call cpt_detect() + autoplot(); experts reach upstream object via $fit. P6 — surprises existing users. 0.1.0 four now 0.2.0 surface keep working unchanged. P7 — Document everything ship (new). feature “done” introduced users look first — README vignette — runnable example, package’s help longer contradicts package’s exports. principle §2 operationalises.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Priority engine 0.3.0, DESCRIPTION deferred README already (incorrectly) claims (§2.2(1)); shipping converts false claim true one. Generalized Functional Pruning Optimal Partitioning (Hocking, Rigaill, Fearnhead & Bourque, 2022) lets user encode prior structural constraints sequence segment means constraint graph: means must alternate --(peak detection genomics), monotone-increasing (isotonic), bounded jumps. minimises penalised cost subject graph empirical cost O(Nlog⁡N)O(N \log N), returning exact constrained segmentation. Wraps: gfpop (gfpop(), graph()). Availability caveat: gfpop currently CRAN; wrapper ships behind requireNamespace() excluded CRAN examples/tests engine installable, claim README restored dependency genuinely available. Proposed: gfpop_wrapper(x, graph = NULL, type = c("std","updown","isotonic","relevant"), penalty = "BIC", ...) → ggcpt. Plot: autoplot() overlays constrained step-mean (show_segments); updown graphs shades /states.

what: 4.2 SMUCE / HSMUCE — multiscale inference with confidence
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Already named cpt_detect() (smuce, hsmuce) unwired. SMUCE (Frick, Munk & Sieling, 2014) estimates step function minimising number changepoints subject multiscale constraint: estimate must pass multiscale test level α\alpha every interval simultaneously. level α\alpha bounds probability -estimating number changepoints, method delivers confidence intervals changepoint locations confidence bands signal. HSMUCE (Pein, Sieling & Munk, 2017) extends heterogeneous (segment-varying) noise. Wraps: stepR (stepFit(), confband(), confint()). Proposed: smuce_wrapper(x, alpha = 0.5, family = c("gauss","hsmuce","poisson"), ...) → ggcpt ci_lower/ci_upper populated. Plot: autoplot() draws step estimate, shaded confidence band signal, changepoint-location CIs via already-shipped geom_cpt_ci() — today engine producing CIs consume. SMUCE feature finally exercises geom.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Achilles heel penalised methods penalty β\beta; cpt_penalty() constructs standard values tell user pick. CROPS (Haynes, Eckley & Fearnhead, 2017) computes optimal segmentations β\beta ranges [βmin,βmax][\beta_{\min}, \beta_{\max}], exploiting number distinct optimal segmentations small found PELT cost. turns penalty selection guess diagnostic. Wraps: changepoint (CROPS penalty option). Proposed: cpt_crops(x, method, change_in, pen_min, pen_max, ...) → ggcpt_path object holding, per penalty interval, number changepoints total unpenalised cost. Plot: autoplot.ggcpt_path() elbow plot (cost vs. number changepoints); ggcpt_pathplot() faceted small-multiple actual segmentation distinct changepoint count, analyst sees models chosen among.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Three engines handle regimes naïve change--mean -counts; three already named (robust, decafs, sn) dispatcher. Robust loss (robseg). Fearnhead & Rigaill (2019): bounded loss robust arbitrary outliers. exact DP minimises 𝒞(ya:b)=min⁡μ∑=abγ((yi−μ)/σ)\mathcal{C}(y_{:b}) = \min_{\mu}\sum_{=}^{b}\gamma\!\big((y_i-\mu)/\sigma\big) γ(u)=min⁡(u2,K2)\gamma(u)=\min(u^2,K^2) (truncated quadratic / biweight), plus Huber L1L_1 variants. robseg_wrapper(x, loss = c("biweight","huber","L1","L2"), lambda = ..., ...). Availability caveat: robseg CRAN; ships behind guard like gfpop. DeCAFS. Romano, Rigaill, Runge & Fearnhead (2022): mean drifts (random walk) abrupt changes noise autocorrelated (AR(1)). DeCAFS minimises ∑t[(yt−μt)2/σ2+(μt−μt−1)2/η2⋅𝟙{change}]+β⋅(#changes)\sum_t\big[(y_t-\mu_t)^2/\sigma^2 + (\mu_t-\mu_{t-1})^2/\eta^2\cdot\mathbb{1}\{\text{change}\}\big] + \beta\cdot(\#\text{changes}). decafs_wrapper(x, beta = ..., model_param = NULL, ...) (DeCAFS CRAN). Self-normalisation (SNSeg). Zhao, Jiang & Shao (2022): SN-based tests nested local-window segmentation; nonparametric, robust dependence, avoids long-run-variance estimation, detects changes general parameters (mean, variance, quantiles, autocovariance, correlation) univariate, multivariate, high-dimensional series. sn_wrapper(x, parameter = c("mean","variance","acf","bivcor"), ...).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: methods surveyed enough mathematics citation directly actionable, scheduled 0.3.0 wave. already named--unwired method cpt_detect() (§1.2), wiring additive.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: KCP (kcpRS). Arlot, Celisse & Harchaoui (2019): map data RKHS via Gaussian kernel minimise within-phase scatter criterion running statistics (mean, variance, autocorrelation, correlation), permutation test existence model selection number. Captures higher-order changes (e.g. correlation) mean/variance methods miss. kcp_wrapper(x, running_stat = c("mean","var","ar","cor"), wsize = 25, ...). Sequential CPM (cpm). Hawkins; Ross (2015): distribution-free sequential detection via repeated two-sample tests — Mann–Whitney (location), Mood (scale), Lepage (location+scale), Kolmogorov–Smirnov Cramér–von-Mises (general). Also runs batch (processStream). cpm_wrapper(x, cpm_type = "Mann-Whitney", arl0 = 500, startup = 20, ...). NMCD / E-Divisive (already shipped via cpt.np / ecp). Expose nquantiles CROPS path cpt.np; lift ecp genuine multivariate (§3.2) expose alpha, sig.lvl, R.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: inspect (InspectChangepoint). Wang & Samworth (2018): pp-variate series whose mean changes unknown sparse subset coordinates, form CUSUM matrix, find projection direction sparse-SVD convex relaxation, project 1-D apply univariate detector, recursing via WBS. inspect_wrapper(X, lambda = NULL, threshold = NULL, ...); plot adds sparse projection direction (coordinates drive change). SBS / double-CUSUM (hdbinseg). Cho & Fryzlewicz (2015); Cho (2016): threshold aggregate per-coordinate CUSUMs suppress noise coordinates segmenting. cpt_detect(method = "sbs"). changepoints collection. Xu, Padilla, Wang, Yu & Li: univariate mean covariance, high-dimensional mean, high-dimensional regression (incl. temporal dependence; Xu, Wang, Zhao & Yu, 2024), dynamic networks, VAR, several confidence procedures. changepoints_wrapper(data, model = c("mean","cov","regression","network","var"), ...).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: bcp. Erdman & Emerson (2007), Barry & Hartigan (1993): product-partition model via MCMC returning posterior probability changepoint every location plus posterior segment means credible intervals. bcp_wrapper(x, ..., prob_threshold = 0.5); changepoints gains posterior_prob column. Plot: posterior means + credible band top, per-location posterior probability . BOCPD (ocp). Adams & MacKay (2007): online posterior run length rtr_t via P(rt,y1:t)=∑rt−1P(rt∣rt−1)P(yt∣rt−1,yt(r))P(rt−1,y1:t−1)P(r_t, y_{1:t}) = \sum_{r_{t-1}} P(r_t\mid r_{t-1})\,P(y_t\mid r_{t-1}, y^{(r)}_t)\,P(r_{t-1}, y_{1:t-1}), hazard H(⋅)H(\cdot) conjugate predictive. bocpd_wrapper(x, hazard_lambda = 250, ...). Plot: run-length posterior heatmap — signature BOCPD graphic.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Bai–Perron / fluctuation tests (strucchange). Zeileis et al. (2002): generalized fluctuation tests detecting instability Bai & Perron (1998, 2003) dynamic program (breakpoints()) dating multiple breaks yt=xt⊤βj+εty_t = x_t^\top\beta_j + \varepsilon_t, CIs break dates. strucchange_wrapper(formula, data, breaks = NULL, ...). segmented. Muggeo (2003, 2008): continuous piecewise-linear (“broken-line”) fits breakpoint standard errors per-segment slopes. segmented_wrapper(model, seg_var, npsi = 1, ...). render fitted regime lines break CIs rather vertical rules (§7.3).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: FOCuS (FOCuS). Romano, Eckley, Fearnhead & Rigaill (2023): online change--mean functional pruning CUSUM likelihood — equivalent running CUSUM simultaneously magnitudes window sizes, amortised log\log cost. focus_wrapper(x, threshold = NULL, ...); streaming batch. (CRAN; guarded.) OCD (ocd). Chen, Wang & Samworth (2022): high-dimensional online mean change, storage per-observation cost independent history, guaranteed average-run-length. ocd_wrapper(X, thresh = "MC", ...).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: B1. signal_blocks() produce Blocks signal; data contradicts true_changepoints. simulate.R level loop runs reverse assigns absolute levels >= mask: iteration reversed, final assignment (= 1, t >= 0.10) overwrites entire tail, collapsing 11 blocks single step first changepoint. Reproduction: 11 declared true_changepoints, 1 shows real jump generated series; ten sit flat noise. worst kind bug benchmark package — “canonical test signal” whose ground truth wrong — every example, test, evaluation built signal_blocks() scored fiction. Fix: iterate forward (seq_along), build cumulative jumps; add test asserting declared changepoint genuine level change. (signals — fms, mix, teeth, stairs — checked correct.) B2. cpt_metrics() double-counts true positives; recall f1 can exceed 1. precision/recall loop matches every prediction nearest truth independently increments tp time, several predictions near one true changepoint count hit. Reproduction: recall > 1, f1 > 1, implied fn = length(truth) - tp goes negative. Fix: one--one matching — truth may claimed one prediction (e.g. greedy nearest matching removal, bipartite match) — tp ≤ min(|pred|, |truth|). covering Rand metrics unaffected (partition-based) stayed [0, 1] testing. B3. cpt_detect() silently changes ignores change_in. Two faces one problem, verified: - change_in = "slope" accepted match.arg() change_in_mapping() maps "mean"; pure linear trend call runs mean-change detection (returning spurious changepoints) resulting object reports change_in = "mean". user’s request silently discarded. - search/pruning engines (fpop, wbs, wbs2, , mosum, idetect, tguh) change_in passed , object hard-coded change_in = "mean". cpt_detect(x, method = "fpop",   change_in = "var") reports mean mean detection. Fix: validate method × change_in combination error (route capable engine — support slope/var contrasts) instead silently mislabelling; record actual change_in object. B4. ecp_wrapper() returns wrong cp_value multivariate input, cpt_detect() flattens . .numeric() TRUE numeric matrix, cp_value <- data[cp] branch fires returns column-major flattened scalar (e.g. X[101]), per-coordinate change; data.frame falls else branch returns NA. Separately, cpt_detect(X, method = "ecp") runs .numeric(X), 200×2 matrix seen length-400 vector. ecp package’s multivariate engine, primary use case. Fix: §3.2 (route multivariate input without flattening; populate segments instead scalar cp_value). B5. stat_changepoint() ignores x aesthetic draws lines raw indices. StatChangepoint$compute_group() returns xintercept = cp$cp (integer positions) regardless panel’s x scale. axis 1:n rules land wrong place. Reproduction: aes(t, y) t spans 2000–2019, changepoint observation 100 drawn xintercept = 100 — visible axis — ggplot also warns x/y aesthetics dropped. Fix: map detected index back x aesthetic (xintercept = data$x[cp$cp]) preserve grouping.

Penalty semantics differ across engines undocumented. cpt_penalty("MBIC", n) returns 2*log(n) (k = 1), changepoint package’s MBIC; numeric penalty means different things fpop (lambda) string "MBIC" PELT. title: Construct changepoint penalties — cpt_penalty
text: penalty name may interpreted differently different engines: changepoint-based methods (PELT, BinSeg, SegNeigh, AMOC):     accept character penalties ("MBIC", "BIC", "AIC",     "Hannan-Quinn", "None") pass upstream     changepoint package. methods accept raw numeric     penalty values. Functional-pruning methods (fpop): accept numeric     penalties . character penalty supplied via     cpt_detect(), resolved numeric value using     cpt_penalty() dispatch. Search-based methods (WBS, WBS2, , MOSUM, IDetect,     TGUH): use internal model-selection criteria (e.g., sSIC, threshold)     generally ignore penalty argument. Specify     thresholds via wrapper's arguments. MBIC cpt_penalty() uses     Zhang-Siegmund (2007) formula \(0.5(k+1)\log n + \log{n \choose k}\),     differs changepoint package's MBIC. Use     character "MBIC" changepoint-based methods get     engine's native MBIC.

title: ggchangepoint package — ggchangepoint-package
text: ggchangepoint provides consistent S3 result class (ggcpt) changepoint detection results, broom-style methods (tidy(), glance(), augment()), ggplot2 integration via autoplot() composable geoms (geom_changepoint(), geom_cpt_segment(), geom_cpt_ci(), stat_changepoint()), unified dispatcher cpt_detect() supports multiple engines. **Detection engines.** package currently wraps 13 methods behind unified cpt_detect() front-end: changepoint: PELT, BINSEG, SEGNEIGH, AMOC (mean, var, meanvar) changepoint.np: NP (non-parametric, distribution-free) ecp: E-Divisive, E-Agglo (multivariate, non-parametric) fpop: FPOP (functional pruning optimal partitioning) wbs: Wild Binary Segmentation breakfast: WBS2, TGUH : Narrowest--Threshold (mean, var, slope contrasts) mosum: Moving Sum IDetect: Isolate-Detect Additional engines planned (see cpt_methods() full table). **Key features.** Every detector returns ggcpt object stable tibble(cp, cp_value) contract. Visualise result directly autoplot(). Compare methods ggcpt_compare() ggcpt_compare_table(). Evaluate accuracy cpt_metrics() ggcpt_eval(). Generate synthetic data known ground truth via cpt_simulate() built-canonical test signals.

title: ggchangepoint 0.3.0
text: README now introduces every exported function, grouped role, -claimed gfpop engine (never wrapped) removed . New feature-tour vignette (vignette("ggchangepoint")) walking full exported surface, including per-engine wrappers, theme_ggcpt(), annotate_segments(). package-level help (?ggchangepoint) rewritten describe unified ggcpt framework current 13-method engine list (previously still claimed “three changepoint packages”). New documentation-coverage test asserting every export appears README.

title: ggchangepoint 0.3.0
text: New cpt_methods() introspection helper returning tibble every known method, engine, availability status, whether engine installed. New S3 methods ggcpt class: summary(), as_tibble(), .data.frame(), format(), plot(). cpt_penalty() gained documented per-engine penalty-semantics section.

title: ggchangepoint 0.3.0
text: cpt_detect() longer advertises 13 methods errored runtime; match.arg() now enumerates wired methods (B7). cpt_detect() validates method × change_in combinations errors clear message instead silently mislabelling result (B3). signal_blocks() now produces correct Blocks signal; segment levels previously collapsed single step assignment loop ran reverse (B1). cpt_metrics() uses one--one matching, recall f1 can longer exceed 1 (B2), longer warns empty pred/truth (B6). ecp_wrapper() returns correct per-coordinate cp_value matrix data.frame input instead column-major flattened scalar (B4); cpt_detect() longer flattens multivariate input passing ecp. stat_changepoint() maps detected indices back x aesthetic rules land correct location non-1:n axes (B5). glance.ggcpt() now reports measured runtime populates total_cost underlying fit available (B8). augment.ggcpt() renames data columns position-independently, longer breaks data carries two columns (B11). cpt_simulate() @return now documents seg_id column actually returns (B9), dead show_segments parameter removed internal plot helper (B10).
